From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Western Christian liturgical season

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Way-of-salvation-church-militant-triumphant-andrea-di-bonaiuto-1365.jpg/330px-Way-of-salvation-church-militant-triumphant-andrea-di-bonaiuto-1365.jpg)](/wiki/File:Way-of-salvation-church-militant-triumphant-andrea-di-bonaiuto-1365.jpg)

*[The Church Militant and the Church Triumphant](/wiki/Church_militant_and_church_triumphant "Church militant and church triumphant")*, fresco by [Andrea da Firenze](/wiki/Andrea_da_Firenze "Andrea da Firenze") in [Santa Maria Novella](/wiki/Basilica_of_Santa_Maria_Novella "Basilica of Santa Maria Novella"), c. 1365

**Allhallowtide**[[a]](#cite_note-5) is the [Western Christian](/wiki/Western_Christian_Church "Western Christian Church") season[[5]](#cite_note-Leslie1895-6) encompassing the [triduum](/wiki/Triduum "Triduum") of [All Hallows' Eve](/wiki/All_Hallows%27_Eve "All Hallows' Eve") (Halloween), [All Hallows' Day](/wiki/All_Hallows%27_Day "All Hallows' Day") (All Saints' Day) and [All Souls' Day](/wiki/All_Souls%27_Day "All Souls' Day"),[[6]](#cite_note-7)[[7]](#cite_note-8)[[8]](#cite_note-9) as well as the [International Day of Prayer for the Persecuted Church](/wiki/International_Day_of_Prayer_for_the_Persecuted_Church "International Day of Prayer for the Persecuted Church") (observed on the first Sunday of November) and [Remembrance Sunday](/wiki/Remembrance_Sunday "Remembrance Sunday") (observed on the second Sunday in November) in some traditions.[[9]](#cite_note-Aleteia2022-10)[[10]](#cite_note-Oremus-11)[[11]](#cite_note-Dyroff-12) The period begins on 31 October annually.[[12]](#cite_note-13) Allhallowtide is a "time to remember the dead, including [martyrs](/wiki/Christian_martyrs "Christian martyrs"), [saints](/wiki/Saint "Saint"), and all faithful departed Christians."[[13]](#cite_note-14) The present date of [Hallowmas](/wiki/Hallowmas "Hallowmas") (All Saints' Day) and thus also of its vigil (Hallowe'en) was established for Rome perhaps by [Pope Gregory III](/wiki/Pope_Gregory_III "Pope Gregory III") (731–741) and was made of obligation throughout the [Frankish Empire](/wiki/Frankish_Empire "Frankish Empire") by [Louis the Pious](/wiki/Louis_the_Pious "Louis the Pious") in 835.[[14]](#cite_note-Britannica-15) Elsewhere, other dates were observed even later, with the date in Ireland being 20 April.[[15]](#cite_note-16) In the early 11th century, the modern date of All Souls' Day was popularized, after Abbot [Odilo](/wiki/Odilo_of_Cluny "Odilo of Cluny") established it as a day for the monks of Cluny and associated monasteries to [pray for the dead](/wiki/Pray_for_the_dead "Pray for the dead").[[16]](#cite_note-17)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Allhallowtide&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The word *Allhallowtide* was first used in 1471,[[17]](#cite_note-18) and is derived from three words: the [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") word *[hallow](/wiki/Hallow "Hallow")*, meaning 'holy', the word *tide*, meaning 'time' or 'season' (cf. [Christmastide](/wiki/Christmastide "Christmastide"), [Eastertide](/wiki/Eastertide "Eastertide")),[[18]](#cite_note-Chambers1998-19) and *all* (from Old English *eall*) meaning "every". The latter part of the word *Hallowmas* is derived from the word *[Mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy) "Mass (liturgy)")*.[[19]](#cite_note-20) The words *hallow* and *saint* are [synonyms](/wiki/Synonyms "Synonyms").[[20]](#cite_note-Diehl-21)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Allhallowtide&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/A-Procession-in-the-Catacomb-of-Callistus.jpg/250px-A-Procession-in-the-Catacomb-of-Callistus.jpg)](/wiki/File:A-Procession-in-the-Catacomb-of-Callistus.jpg)

Many of the remains of the [martyrs](/wiki/Christian_martyrs "Christian martyrs") of the [ancient Church](/wiki/Ancient_Church "Ancient Church") lie in the [catacombs](/wiki/Catacombs_of_Rome "Catacombs of Rome")[[21]](#cite_note-Jeremiah2012-22)

The Christian attitude toward the death of [martyrs](/wiki/Christian_martyr "Christian martyr") is first exemplified in the [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament"), which records that after the [beheading of St. John the Baptist](/wiki/Beheading_of_St._John_the_Baptist "Beheading of St. John the Baptist"), his disciples respectfully buried him.[[22]](#cite_note-Publishing1986-23) [Stephen](/wiki/Stephen "Stephen") was likewise "given a [Christian burial](/wiki/Christian_burial "Christian burial") by his fellow-Christians after he had been stoned to death by a mob."[[23]](#cite_note-Concordia1919-24) Two of the Post-Nicene Fathers, [Ephrem the Syrian](/wiki/Ephrem_the_Syrian "Ephrem the Syrian"),[[24]](#cite_note-SmithWace1880-25) as well as [John Chrysostom](/wiki/John_Chrysostom "John Chrysostom"),[[25]](#cite_note-Tassone2001-26) both wrote about the importance of honoring the dead; the theologian [Herman Heuser](/wiki/Herman_Heuser "Herman Heuser") writes that in the [early Church](/wiki/Early_Church "Early Church"), the feast days of the martyrs were local observances,[[26]](#cite_note-Heuser1934-27) with [churches](/wiki/Church_(building) "Church (building)") being built on those sites where their blood was shed.[[27]](#cite_note-Webb2001-28) Frances Stewart Mossier explains that this changed during the [persecution of Christians in the Roman Empire](/wiki/Persecution_of_Christians_in_the_Roman_Empire "Persecution of Christians in the Roman Empire"), saying that:

> This arrangement worked very well at first, but soon there were more martyrs than there were days in the year, and so one day was set apart in honor of them all, and called All Saints' Day. This took place about the year A.D. 610. The day of the year on which the festival first occurred was the first of May, and it was not till two hundred years after that it was changed to Nov. 1, the day we now observe. The Christians of those times were in the habit of spending the night before All Saints' Day in thinking over the good and helpful lives of those in whose honor the day was kept and in praying that they might be like them. Services were held in the churches, and candles and [incense](/wiki/Religious_use_of_incense "Religious use of incense") burned before the pictures and statues of the saints. It was to them one of the holiest, most significant days of all the year.[[28]](#cite_note-Mossier-29)

Following the establishment of All Hallows' Day and its [vigil](/wiki/Vigil "Vigil"), All Hallows' Eve in the 8th century,[[14]](#cite_note-Britannica-15) [Odilo of Cluny](/wiki/Odilo_of_Cluny "Odilo of Cluny") popularized the day to pray for [All Souls](/wiki/All_Souls%27_Day "All Souls' Day"),[[29]](#cite_note-Farmer2011-30) forming the third day of the [triduum](/wiki/Triduum "Triduum") of Allhallowtide.[[30]](#cite_note-31) It has been thought that the first three days of Allhallowtide may have originated as a ritualistic remembrance of the [deluge](/wiki/Flood_myth "Flood myth") in which the first night, [All Hallows' Eve](/wiki/Halloween "Halloween") remembers the wickedness of the world before flood. The second night then celebrates the saved who survived the deluge and the third night celebrates those who would repopulate the Earth.[[31]](#cite_note-32)

The [octave](/wiki/Octave_(liturgical) "Octave (liturgical)") of Allhallowtide,[[32]](#cite_note-33) lasting "eight days was established by [Pope Sixtus IV](/wiki/Pope_Sixtus_IV "Pope Sixtus IV") in 1430 for the whole Western Church."[[33]](#cite_note-Pfatteicher1990-34): 322 The octave, however was eliminated in the [1955 reforms](/wiki/Liturgical_reforms_of_Pope_Pius_XII "Liturgical reforms of Pope Pius XII") of the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"),[[34]](#cite_note-EisenhoferLechner1961-35) although it continues to be observed by many Lutherans and Anglicans.[[33]](#cite_note-Pfatteicher1990-34): 301[[35]](#cite_note-36) The faithful may still obtain a [Plenary Indulgence](/wiki/Plenary_indulgence "Plenary indulgence") by visiting a cemetery and [praying for the dead](/wiki/Praying_for_the_dead "Praying for the dead") during the octave of All Hallows.[[36]](#cite_note-37) Within Allhallowtide, which has a theme revolving around martyrs and saints, many Christian denominations also observe the [International Day of Prayer for the Persecuted Church](/wiki/International_Day_of_Prayer_for_the_Persecuted_Church "International Day of Prayer for the Persecuted Church") on the first Sunday of November, to remember those who continue to be [persecuted for their Christian faith](/wiki/Persecution_of_Christians_in_the_modern_era "Persecution of Christians in the modern era").[[9]](#cite_note-Aleteia2022-10)[[37]](#cite_note-CT2019-38)[[38]](#cite_note-Henry-Crowe2018-39)[[11]](#cite_note-Dyroff-12) In the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"), [mother church](/wiki/Mother_church "Mother church") of the [Anglican Communion](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion"), extended All Saints-tide to include [Remembrance Sunday](/wiki/Remembrance_Sunday "Remembrance Sunday") in the 20th century.[[10]](#cite_note-Oremus-11)

## Triduum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Allhallowtide&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Triduum")]

### All Hallows' Eve

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Allhallowtide&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: All Hallows' Eve")]

Main article: [Halloween](/wiki/Halloween "Halloween")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Hallowe%27en_beim_Eifeler_Hof_-_geo.hlipp.de_-_6914.jpg/250px-Hallowe%27en_beim_Eifeler_Hof_-_geo.hlipp.de_-_6914.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hallowe%27en_beim_Eifeler_Hof_-_geo.hlipp.de_-_6914.jpg)

Hallowe'en decorations in Eifeler Hof, North Rhine-Westphalia, [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany").

All Hallows' Eve, often contracted as Halloween, is the eve of [All Hallows' Day](/wiki/All_Hallows%27_Day "All Hallows' Day") (All Saints' Day),[[39]](#cite_note-40)[[40]](#cite_note-41) and the first day of the Allhallowtide.[[41]](#cite_note-Ebony-42) According to some scholars, the [Christian Church](/wiki/Christian_Church "Christian Church") absorbed some Celtic practices associated with [Samhain](/wiki/Samhain "Samhain") and [Christianised](/wiki/Christianised "Christianised") the celebration in order to ease the Celts' [conversion to Christianity](/wiki/Conversion_to_Christianity "Conversion to Christianity");[[42]](#cite_note-BBC1-43)[[43]](#cite_note-Christianity1-44) other scholars maintain that the Christian observance of All Hallows' Eve arose completely independent of Samhain.[[44]](#cite_note-BBC2-45)[[45]](#cite_note-46)[[46]](#cite_note-47)[[47]](#cite_note-2005Hörandner-48)[[48]](#cite_note-49) On All Hallows' Eve, some believed that the veil between the material world and the [afterlife](/wiki/Christian_eschatology "Christian eschatology") thinned.[[49]](#cite_note-50) In order to prevent recognition by a [soul](/wiki/Soul_in_the_Bible "Soul in the Bible"), "people would don masks or costumes to disguise their identities"; in [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America"), this tradition is perpetuated through the practice of [trick or treating](/wiki/Trick_or_treating "Trick or treating").[[50]](#cite_note-51) In medieval [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland"), believers were taught to [pray](/wiki/Christian_prayer "Christian prayer") out loud as they walk through the forests in order that the souls of the dead might find comfort; in [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain"), Christian priests tolled their [church bells](/wiki/Church_bell "Church bell") in order to allow their congregants to remember the dead on All Hallows' Eve.[[51]](#cite_note-52)

The [Christian Church](/wiki/Christian_Church "Christian Church") traditionally observed Hallowe'en through a [vigil](/wiki/Vigil "Vigil") "when worshippers would prepare themselves with prayers and fasting prior to the feast day itself."[[52]](#cite_note-BBC3-53) This [church service](/wiki/Church_service "Church service") is known as the *Vigil of All Hallows* or the *Vigil of All Saints*;[[53]](#cite_note-Harvey-54)[[54]](#cite_note-CNA-55) an initiative known as *Night of Light* seeks to further spread the observance of *Vigil of All Hallows* throughout [Christendom](/wiki/Christendom "Christendom").[[55]](#cite_note-56)[[56]](#cite_note-57) After the service, "suitable festivities and entertainments" often follow, as well as a visit to the graveyard or [cemetery](/wiki/Cemetery "Cemetery"), where flowers and candles are often placed in preparation for All Saints' Day (All Hallows' Day).[[57]](#cite_note-Armentrout-58)[[58]](#cite_note-Infeld-59)

### All Hallows' Day

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Allhallowtide&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: All Hallows' Day")]

Main article: [All Saints' Day](/wiki/All_Saints%27_Day "All Saints' Day")

See also: [Day of the Dead](/wiki/Day_of_the_Dead "Day of the Dead")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Alla_helgons_dag_vid_R%C3%B6ke_kyrka-1.jpg/250px-Alla_helgons_dag_vid_R%C3%B6ke_kyrka-1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alla_helgons_dag_vid_R%C3%B6ke_kyrka-1.jpg)

A cemetery outside an [Evangelical Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") church ([Church of Sweden](/wiki/Church_of_Sweden "Church of Sweden")) in [Röke](/wiki/R%C3%B6ke "Röke"), [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") on the feast of All Hallows. Flowers and lighted candles are placed by relatives on the graves of their deceased loved ones.

The second day of Allhallowtide is known as All Saints' Day, All Hallows' Day, or Hallowmas.[[59]](#cite_note-BBC4-60) Occurring on 1 November, it is a "principal feast of the [church year](/wiki/Christian_Year "Christian Year"), and one of the four days recommended for the administration of baptism" in [Anglicanism](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism").[[60]](#cite_note-Date-61) In some [Christian denominations](/wiki/Christian_denominations "Christian denominations"), All Saints' Day may be "celebrated on the Sunday following November 1."[[60]](#cite_note-Date-61) All Saints' Day is a holy day to honour all the [saints](/wiki/Saints "Saints") and [martyrs](/wiki/Martyrs "Martyrs"), both known and unknown.[[59]](#cite_note-BBC4-60)[[61]](#cite_note-Granieri-62) All Hallows' Day is "a universal Christian [holy day](/wiki/Liturgical_year "Liturgical year"),"[[61]](#cite_note-Granieri-62) but it has a special importance in the [Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church"), [Evangelical Lutheran churches](/wiki/Lutheran_Church "Lutheran Church"), [Anglican Church](/wiki/Anglican_Church "Anglican Church"), and some other [Protestant churches](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism").[[62]](#cite_note-63) The [liturgical colour](/wiki/Liturgical_colour "Liturgical colour") of All Saints' Day is white, which is "symbolic of victory and life."[[20]](#cite_note-Diehl-21)[[63]](#cite_note-Weaver-64) While honouring the [Church Triumphant](/wiki/Church_Triumphant "Church Triumphant"), All Hallows' Day seeks to especially "honour the blessed who have not been canonized and who have no special feast day."[[64]](#cite_note-65) On All Saints' Day, many Christians visit [graveyards](/wiki/Graveyard "Graveyard") and cemeteries in order to place flowers and candles on the graves of their loved ones.[[65]](#cite_note-Hannam-66) This is a common practice in countries such as [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain"), [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland"), the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), as well as certain parts of the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") heavily influenced by [Roman Catholicism](/wiki/Roman_Catholicism "Roman Catholicism") such as [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana "Louisiana") and [Maryland](/wiki/Maryland "Maryland").[[65]](#cite_note-Hannam-66)[[66]](#cite_note-67) For [Roman Catholic Christians](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), attending [Mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy) "Mass (liturgy)") ([Eucharist](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist"), Holy Communion, "Lord's Supper") is compulsory, as All Saints' Day (All Hallows' Day) is a [holy day of obligation](/wiki/Holy_day_of_obligation "Holy day of obligation");[[67]](#cite_note-68) for members of other [Christian denominations](/wiki/Christian_denominations "Christian denominations"), such as the [Evangelical Lutheran Church](/wiki/Lutheran_Church "Lutheran Church"), [Anglican Church / Episcopal Church](/wiki/Anglican_Church "Anglican Church"), [Methodist Church](/wiki/United_Methodist_Church "United Methodist Church") and some other [Protestant Christians](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism"), though not mandatory, attendance at [worship services](/wiki/Church_service "Church service") is encouraged.[[59]](#cite_note-BBC4-60)[[68]](#cite_note-Methodism-69)

### All Souls' Day

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Allhallowtide&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: All Souls' Day")]

Main article: [All Souls' Day](/wiki/All_Souls%27_Day "All Souls' Day")

See also: [Day of the Dead](/wiki/Day_of_the_Dead "Day of the Dead")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Jakub_Schikaneder_-_All_Souls%27_Day.jpg/250px-Jakub_Schikaneder_-_All_Souls%27_Day.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jakub_Schikaneder_-_All_Souls%27_Day.jpg)

*[All Souls' Day](/wiki/All_Souls%27_Day "All Souls' Day")*, [J Schikaneder](/wiki/Jakub_Schikaneder "Jakub Schikaneder") 1888. This oil painting shows an elderly woman praying after placing a wreath upon the [tombstone](/wiki/Tombstone "Tombstone") of her loved one.

The final day of Allhallowtide is known as All Souls' Day,[[41]](#cite_note-Ebony-42) and is also called the Commemoration of All Faithful Departed.[[69]](#cite_note-Third-70) All Souls' Day focuses on honouring *all* faithful Christians "who are unknown in the wider fellowship of the church, especially family members and friends."[[69]](#cite_note-Third-70) However, today, All Saints' Day and All Souls' Day have become conflated, and many Christians remember all the dead souls or "saints" on All Saints' Day.'[[70]](#cite_note-71) The observance of All Souls' Day "was spread throughout Europe" by [Saint Odilo of Cluny](/wiki/Saint_Odilo_of_Cluny "Saint Odilo of Cluny") in the early 11th century.[[71]](#cite_note-Odilo-72) Like All Hallows' Eve and All Saints' Day (All Hallows' Day), family members often attend [mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy) "Mass (liturgy)") and visit the graves of their deceased loved ones, placing flowers and lighted candles there.[[71]](#cite_note-Odilo-72)[[72]](#cite_note-BBC5-73) In many [Anglican](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism") / [Episcopal](/wiki/Episcopal_Church_(United_States) "Episcopal Church (United States)"), [Evangelical Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") and [Roman Catholic Christian](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") services, an A.D. 7th-century prayer *[The Office of the Dead](/wiki/The_Office_of_the_Dead "The Office of the Dead")* is read out in churches on All Souls' Day."[[72]](#cite_note-BBC5-73) In [England](/wiki/England "England"), a popular tradition associated with All Souls' Day is [souling](/wiki/Soul_cake "Soul cake"), in which "bands of children, or of poor men, went round to the houses of the well-to-do on Souling Day, as they called it, begging money, apples, ale, or doles of cake. In some parts specially baked cakes were prepared in readiness to give away; they were called [soul-cakes](/wiki/Soul_cake "Soul cake")."[[73]](#cite_note-Hackwood-74) The individuals who go souling often chant rhymes as they go door to door; for example, an old saying goes:[[73]](#cite_note-Hackwood-74) "A Soule-cake, a soule-cake, have mercy on all Christian souls for a soule-cake."[[74]](#cite_note-Walford-75) Historically, in [France](/wiki/France "France"), on All Souls' Day, "the burial fraternities were especially active in decorating the churchyard, and everywhere priests led a procession around the graveyard and blessed the graves."[[75]](#cite_note-76)

"All Souls' Day is said to have its origins in an ancient festival of the dead and was spread through Europe by St. Odilo of France in the eleventh century. It was celebrated on November 2 with masses and celebrations in honor of the dead. It is a time when families fondly remember their deceased members and pray for their souls. Many people also visit their loved ones' graves bearing flowers."
